result:
There’s a particular moment, just before dusk, when the world hums with an almost audible anticipation. The sun hovers low enough to cast long shadows that stretch like soft-stretched taffy across grasses, pavement, walls. Birds weave languid arcs through the air, their songs neither day’s exuberance nor night’s mystery—something in between. I find myself coming back to this time of day again and again, not because anything profound necessarily happens then, but because it’s a space that’s inherently undecided. Twilight is late afternoon’s ellipsis: a pause, a breath that hasn’t yet drawn in the dark and hasn’t yet let go of the light.

I like these in-between spaces. They’re gentle reminders that we, too, spend much of our lives in softly transitional states, even if the world loves to talk in conclusions and milestones. Think of all the “somedays” we live in: studying toward a degree that isn’t yet ours, dating someone but not yet married, waiting on a job offer, still contemplating whether to move cities, practicing guitar quietly at night, not sure who will ever hear us play. Most of life’s texture lies there—between definitions and the identity labels we might eventually wear.

We’re not very patient with these in-between states. We want to resolve the musical chord to a neat major triad, to tie off the loose ends and call it finished. But the in-between has a underrated beauty. It teaches us to hold multiple truths simultaneously: you can be both uncertain and still moving forward, both hesitant and quietly brave. While you’re a student, you’re also already a thinker; while you’re a beginner guitarist, you’re already a musician in motion. The identity is not deferred until the certificate or the wedding or the big audience. It’s breathing within you.

Here’s something I’ve learned while contemplating twilights and in-between-ness: the process isn’t a waiting room for some “real” life. The question “When will this be done?” usually isn’t as helpful as “Who am I becoming while I do this?” Because even if the outcome veers sideways—say the job doesn’t land, the move doesn’t happen, or the book manuscript never quite resolves—the becoming, the small incremental change in how we think and feel and treat people, arrives regardless. If enlightenment existed, maybe it’d simply be the practice of savoring these incremental metamorphoses without demanding they arrive in headline-grabbing bursts.

There’s a paradox to cherish: the more we allow ourselves to inhabit the in-between, the richer and more serene the rest of life becomes. The bird at dusk is neither in frantic flight nor at silent roost. It is at ease in the transition. When I remind myself to do the same, I notice different things: the smell of dinner drifting through open windows, the subtle color shift as the sky sighs into lavender, the slight coolness that hints at the evening ahead. I become aware of my own breath, how it slows in the presence of stillness.

Imagine if we approached our unfinished projects, uncertain relationships, and half-formed ideas with the same gentle attention. Not pressing them to hurry into full bloom, but tending them like twilight gardeners, trusting that whether they flower tomorrow or never quite as we imagined, the tending itself shapes us. Perhaps that’s the invitation dusk extends each day: to dwell comfortably in the unresolved, to love the becoming as much as the being.

So here’s to the liminal hours and liminal lives. To the stories being written sentence by sentence, even when no tidy ending is in sight. To the learners, the try-ers, the people folding new habits into old routines. To a world constantly drafting itself anew, especially in that soft glow between daylight and starlight, where nothing has to be decided yet—and yet everything feels quietly full of promise.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
